name: Rash
summary: Evaluation of acute or subacute non-emergent skin eruptions in ambulatory adults.

triage: Vitals; assess for fever or systemic symptoms
Onset, distribution, new exposures (drugs, foods, plants, products)
Screen for airway/throat swelling, wheeze, or hypotension (anaphylaxis)
Ask about mucosal involvement, blistering, or skin sloughing
nursing: Document distribution, morphology, and percent body surface area
Note prior similar episodes and treatments tried
keyOrders: POC glucose if diabetic with skin findings*
Wood lamp exam if fungal suspected* if indicated
labs: Usually none for uncomplicated rash
Rapid strep if scarlatiniform rash with pharyngitis*
KOH prep if available for suspected tinea* if indicated
Defer biopsy and serologies to dermatology/outpatient
meds: Cetirizine 10 mg PO daily or diphenhydramine 25-50 mg PO for itch
Topical hydrocortisone 1% or triamcinolone 0.1% BID for localized inflammation
Topical antifungal (clotrimazole) BID if tinea*
Short oral prednisone taper for widespread allergic dermatitis* if indicated
reassess: Recheck for spreading, systemic symptoms, or airway change
Confirm patient tolerates first antihistamine dose
Re-evaluate working diagnosis before discharge
disposition: Discharge home with symptomatic care for most localized rashes
PCP or dermatology referral if persistent, unclear, or chronic
Transfer to ER or call 911 if airway swelling, anaphylaxis, mucosal involvement, blistering/sloughing, or petechiae with fever
discharge: Avoid suspected trigger; use fragrance-free moisturizers
Return for spreading, fever, blistering, or breathing difficulty
Take antihistamines as directed; warn re sedation with diphenhydramine
followup: PCP in 1-2 weeks if not resolving
Dermatology referral for recurrent or undiagnosed rash
Sooner if worsening

redFlags: Anaphylaxis: airway swelling, wheeze, hypotension
Mucosal involvement, blistering, or skin sloughing (SJS/TEN)
Purpura/petechiae with fever (meningococcemia)
Rapidly spreading rash with systemic toxicity
compliance: Urgent care manages low-acuity rashes; refer complex/chronic dermatoses out
EMTALA-style transfer to ER for suspected SJS/TEN, anaphylaxis, or sepsis
Document return precautions and trigger avoidance
Urgent care is not a freestanding ER

name: Mild Cellulitis
summary: Outpatient management of nonpurulent, uncomplicated cellulitis without systemic toxicity.

triage: Vitals; flag fever, tachycardia, or hypotension
Mark borders of erythema with skin marker and time
Assess for rapid spread, severe pain, or crepitus
Screen diabetes, immunosuppression, and PAD
nursing: Measure and outline area of erythema/warmth
Check for portal of entry (tinea pedis, wound, ulcer)
keyOrders: Wound culture if drainage present* if indicated
labs: Often none for mild cellulitis
POC glucose in diabetics
Defer CBC/CRP/blood cultures to ER if systemic illness suspected
imaging: Plain X-ray if foreign body or gas in tissue suspected* if indicated
Defer ultrasound/CT to ER for abscess vs necrotizing concern
meds: Cephalexin 500 mg PO QID x 5-7 days (nonpurulent)
Dicloxacillin 500 mg PO QID as alternative
Clindamycin 300-450 mg PO QID if penicillin-allergic
Add MRSA coverage (TMP-SMX or doxycycline) if purulent features*
reassess: Recheck demarcated borders for progression
Confirm patient stable and able to take oral meds
Reassess pain out of proportion (necrotizing concern)
disposition: Discharge home on oral antibiotics with limb elevation
PCP follow-up to confirm improvement in 48 hours
Transfer to ER or call 911 if systemic toxicity, rapid spread, crepitus, pain out of proportion, or need for IV antibiotics
discharge: Complete full antibiotic course; elevate limb
Re-outline marked border; return if spreads beyond it
Return for fever, worsening pain, or no improvement in 48 hours
followup: Recheck in 48 hours (urgent care or PCP)
PCP for recurrent cellulitis workup
Treat predisposing tinea pedis

redFlags: Pain out of proportion, crepitus, bullae, or skin necrosis (necrotizing fasciitis)
Systemic toxicity: high fever, hypotension, confusion (sepsis)
Rapidly advancing erythema
Immunocompromised or facial/orbital involvement
compliance: Urgent care treats mild, nonpurulent cellulitis without systemic signs
EMTALA-style transfer to ER for sepsis or necrotizing infection
Document border marking and 48-hour recheck plan
Antibiotic stewardship per local resistance patterns

name: Cutaneous Abscess
summary: Incision and drainage of simple superficial skin abscesses in ambulatory adults.

triage: Vitals; screen for fever or systemic signs
Assess size, fluctuance, and location
Flag high-risk sites (face, hand, genital, perirectal)
Screen diabetes and immunosuppression
nursing: Prepare I&D tray and local anesthetic
keyOrders: Incision and drainage with packing if cavity large
labs: Wound culture if purulent (especially recurrent/severe)
POC glucose in diabetics
Defer CBC/blood cultures to ER if systemic illness
imaging: Bedside ultrasound to confirm fluid collection if available* if indicated
Defer CT for deep/complex collections to ER
meds: Local anesthetic (lidocaine 1% with epinephrine, avoid digits/end-arteries)
I&D is primary therapy; antibiotics not always needed
TMP-SMX DS 1-2 tabs PO BID or doxycycline 100 mg BID if surrounding cellulitis or risk factors
Acetaminophen/ibuprofen for pain
reassess: Confirm adequate drainage and hemostasis
Verify patient understands packing/wound care
Reassess for spreading cellulitis
disposition: Discharge home after I&D with wound-care instructions
Wound recheck/packing removal in 24-48 hours
Transfer to ER or call 911 if deep/complex abscess, perirectal/facial danger zone, systemic toxicity, or requires OR drainage
discharge: Keep dressing clean and dry; warm compresses
Return for fever, increasing redness, or worsening pain
Complete antibiotics if prescribed
followup: Wound check in 24-48 hours for packing removal
PCP for recurrent abscesses (consider MRSA decolonization)
Surgery referral if complex

redFlags: Perirectal, facial danger triangle, or hand/deep space abscess
Systemic toxicity or surrounding necrosis
Immunocompromised host with rapidly enlarging collection
Suspected deep or septic-joint involvement
compliance: Urgent care performs simple I&D within scope; refer complex sites out
Verify and update tetanus prophylaxis
EMTALA-style transfer to ER for deep/danger-zone abscess or sepsis
Document procedure, consent, and follow-up plan

name: Insect Bite / Sting
summary: Management of local reactions to insect bites/stings and screening for systemic allergy.

triage: Vitals; screen immediately for anaphylaxis
Identify insect/exposure and time of sting
Assess for airway/throat tightness, wheeze, or hives spreading
Note prior systemic reactions
keyOrders: Tetanus update if skin broken* if indicated
labs: None for typical local reaction
Defer allergy testing to allergist outpatient
meds: Cetirizine 10 mg PO daily or diphenhydramine 25-50 mg PO for itch
Topical hydrocortisone 1% or triamcinolone for local swelling
Acetaminophen/ibuprofen for pain
IM epinephrine 0.3 mg if anaphylaxis develops, then transfer*
reassess: Observe for delayed systemic reaction (15-30 min)
Recheck airway and vitals if any allergic symptoms
Confirm large local reactions are not cellulitis
disposition: Discharge home with antihistamines and wound care
Prescribe epinephrine auto-injector and allergist referral if systemic reaction history
Transfer to ER or call 911 if anaphylaxis, airway compromise, or hypotension
discharge: Cold compress, antihistamine, avoid scratching
Return for spreading redness, fever, or breathing difficulty
Carry epinephrine auto-injector if prescribed
followup: PCP if signs of secondary infection
Allergist if systemic reaction occurred
Return precautions reviewed

redFlags: Anaphylaxis: airway swelling, wheeze, hypotension, diffuse hives
Signs of secondary cellulitis or necrotic spider bite
Multiple stings with systemic toxicity
Suspected venomous bite needing antivenom (ER)
compliance: Urgent care manages local reactions and gives epinephrine for anaphylaxis then transfers
EMTALA-style transfer to ER/911 for anaphylaxis or systemic envenomation
Prescribe auto-injector and document return precautions
Update tetanus when skin barrier broken

name: Contact Dermatitis / Poison Ivy
summary: Treatment of localized to moderate allergic/irritant contact dermatitis in adults.

triage: Vitals; assess distribution and percent body surface area
Identify exposure (plants, metals, chemicals, cosmetics)
Screen for facial/genital or extensive involvement
Note prior episodes and treatments
nursing: Advise washing skin and clothing to remove allergen
labs: None routinely
Defer patch testing to dermatology/allergist
meds: Mid/high-potency topical steroid (triamcinolone 0.1% or clobetasol) BID for localized disease
Oral prednisone taper (e.g., 40-60 mg tapered over 14-21 days) for severe/extensive
Cetirizine 10 mg PO daily or hydroxyzine for itch
Cool oatmeal baths and emollients
reassess: Confirm extent and rule out superinfection
Ensure understanding of allergen avoidance
Reassess if facial/periorbital swelling present
disposition: Discharge home with topical/oral steroids and antihistamines
PCP/dermatology if severe, recurrent, or unclear trigger
Transfer to ER if airway involvement, severe facial swelling, or signs of systemic infection
discharge: Wash exposed clothing/tools to remove urushiol
Complete full steroid taper; avoid identified trigger
Return for spreading infection, fever, or worsening swelling
followup: PCP in 1-2 weeks if not improving
Dermatology/allergist for recurrent dermatitis
Sooner if secondary infection

redFlags: Periorbital/facial swelling threatening airway or vision
Signs of secondary bacterial infection (impetiginization, fever)
Extensive blistering mimicking burn
Systemic symptoms suggesting another diagnosis
compliance: Urgent care manages routine contact dermatitis within scope
Use short steroid courses prudently; counsel on tapering
EMTALA-style transfer for airway-threatening facial involvement
Document trigger avoidance and return precautions

name: Sunburn
summary: Supportive care of first/superficial second-degree UV burns without systemic compromise.

triage: Vitals; assess hydration and percent body surface area
Screen for dehydration, syncope, or heat illness
Assess blistering (superficial partial thickness)
Note medications that increase photosensitivity
nursing: Document BSA and degree of burn
Assess for signs of heat exhaustion
keyOrders: POC glucose if syncopal/dehydrated* if indicated
labs: None for routine sunburn
Defer electrolytes to ER if severe dehydration/heat illness
meds: Ibuprofen 400-600 mg PO q6-8h for pain/inflammation
Aloe vera or fragrance-free emollients topically
Topical hydrocortisone 1% for mild inflammation
Oral fluids; avoid breaking blisters
reassess: Recheck hydration status and vitals
Reassess for heat illness if febrile/dizzy
Confirm pain controlled with oral analgesics
disposition: Discharge home with supportive care
PCP if extensive blistering or slow healing
Transfer to ER if signs of heat stroke, large-BSA partial-thickness burn, or severe dehydration
discharge: Cool compresses, moisturizers, hydration, NSAIDs
Avoid sun; use SPF 30+ once healed
Return for fever, infection of blisters, or dizziness
followup: PCP if not improving in several days
Wound check if blisters become infected
Counsel on future sun protection

redFlags: Heat stroke: confusion, high core temperature, collapse
Severe dehydration or syncope
Extensive partial-thickness burn (large BSA)
Signs of secondary infection of blistered skin
compliance: Urgent care manages minor sunburn; large/deep burns or heat stroke go to ER
EMTALA-style transfer to ER/911 for heat stroke or severe dehydration
Document BSA, hydration, and return precautions
Counsel on photosensitizing medications

name: Laceration Repair
summary: Closure of simple, uncomplicated lacerations with intact neurovascular and tendon function.

triage: Vitals; control active bleeding with pressure
Time and mechanism of injury (clean vs contaminated)
Screen for arterial bleeding, deep structure injury
Verify tetanus status
nursing: Apply direct pressure; assess distal neurovascular function
Prepare closure tray and local anesthetic
Document wound length, depth, and contamination
keyOrders: Wound irrigation and exploration for foreign body/tendon involvement
Local anesthesia and primary closure (sutures, staples, adhesive, or strips)
labs: None routine
POC glucose if diabetic with wound* if indicated
imaging: Plain X-ray if radiopaque foreign body (glass/metal) suspected*
Defer to ER for suspected deep/joint/tendon involvement requiring imaging
meds: Lidocaine 1% (with epinephrine except digits/end-arteries) for local anesthesia
Acetaminophen/ibuprofen for pain
Topical antibiotic ointment under dressing
Oral antibiotics only for high-risk/contaminated wounds* if indicated
reassess: Confirm hemostasis and wound edge approximation
Recheck distal neurovascular status post-repair
Verify tetanus given if due
disposition: Discharge home with wound-care and suture-removal timeline
Hand/plastics referral for cosmetic or functional concerns
Transfer to ER or call 911 if uncontrolled bleeding, arterial injury, tendon/nerve involvement, or deep contaminated wounds beyond scope
discharge: Keep wound clean and dry 24-48 hours; then gentle cleansing
Suture removal timeline (face 3-5d, scalp/trunk 7-10d, extremities 10-14d)
Return for redness, drainage, fever, or wound dehiscence
followup: Wound check or suture removal at urgent care/PCP per site
Specialist referral if function or cosmesis at risk
Sooner if infection signs

redFlags: Uncontrolled or pulsatile (arterial) bleeding
Tendon, nerve, joint, or deep structure involvement
Heavily contaminated, bite, or crush wounds
Wounds over 8-12 hours old or with significant tissue loss
compliance: Urgent care repairs simple lacerations; refer functional/cosmetic-critical sites
Mandatory tetanus assessment and documentation
EMTALA-style transfer to ER for vascular/tendon/nerve injury
Document neurovascular exam before and after repair; obtain consent

name: Minor Burn
summary: Care of small superficial or superficial partial-thickness burns in non-critical areas.

triage: Vitals; estimate BSA and depth
Mechanism (thermal, chemical, electrical) and time
Screen for airway/inhalation injury, large BSA, or critical-area burns
Verify tetanus status
nursing: Cool burn with room-temp water; remove jewelry/clothing
Gentle cleansing; do not rupture intact blisters
labs: None for minor burns
Defer labs to ER for large/electrical/chemical burns
imaging: Not indicated for minor thermal burns
meds: Acetaminophen/ibuprofen for pain
Topical antibiotic (bacitracin) or silver sulfadiazine (avoid on face) under dressing
Non-adherent dressing with petrolatum gauze
Oral opioid for short-term severe pain* if indicated
reassess: Reassess depth (may evolve) and pain control
Confirm BSA remains within urgent-care scope
Verify tetanus addressed
disposition: Discharge home with dressing and wound-care plan
Burn-clinic referral for deeper or slow-healing burns
Transfer to ER or burn center if large BSA, full-thickness, face/hands/feet/perineum/joints, circumferential, electrical/chemical, or inhalation injury
discharge: Daily dressing changes; keep clean and dry
Do not pop blisters; use prescribed topical
Return for increasing pain, drainage, fever, or spreading redness
followup: Wound check in 24-48 hours
Burn clinic/plastics if not healing or deep
PCP as needed

redFlags: Burns to face, hands, feet, perineum, or over joints
Large BSA, full-thickness, or circumferential burns
Electrical, chemical, or suspected inhalation injury
Signs of infection or inadequate pain control
compliance: Urgent care treats small superficial burns; critical-area/large burns go to ER or burn center
Mandatory tetanus assessment
EMTALA-style transfer to ER/burn center per ABA referral criteria
Report suspected abuse-related burns per Texas mandatory reporting

name: Wound Check / Suture Removal
summary: Routine follow-up of healing wounds and removal of sutures/staples in stable patients.

triage: Vitals; screen for fever or signs of infection
Confirm original injury date and closure type
Assess pain, drainage, and wound integrity
Review tetanus status from index visit
nursing: Inspect wound for approximation, erythema, drainage
keyOrders: Wound culture if purulent drainage* if indicated
labs: None routinely
Wound culture only if infected* if indicated
POC glucose if diabetic with poor healing* if indicated
imaging: Not indicated unless retained foreign body suspected* if indicated
meds: Topical antibiotic ointment after removal if needed
Adhesive strips to support wound edges post-removal
Acetaminophen/ibuprofen for mild discomfort
Oral antibiotics if early infection identified* if indicated
reassess: Confirm wound edges stable after removal
Reassess for dehiscence or infection
Verify patient comfortable with ongoing care
disposition: Discharge home after removal and wound check
PCP/specialist referral if delayed healing or complication
Transfer to ER if deep infection, abscess, or significant dehiscence beyond scope
discharge: Keep area clean; apply adhesive strips until they fall off
Protect scar from sun; use SPF once healed
Return for redness, drainage, fever, or wound reopening
followup: PCP if scar concerns or delayed healing
Recheck if infection signs develop
Specialist if functional/cosmetic issue

redFlags: Spreading erythema, purulent drainage, or fever (infection)
Significant wound dehiscence
Underlying abscess or deep-space infection
Poor healing in immunocompromised/diabetic patient
compliance: Routine wound checks and suture removal are within urgent-care scope
EMTALA-style transfer for deep infection or significant dehiscence
Document healing status and return precautions
Coordinate with original treating provider when appropriate

name: Animal / Human Bite (Low-Risk)
summary: Care of superficial low-risk bites without deep-structure injury or systemic infection.

triage: Vitals; screen for systemic infection
Biting source, provoked vs unprovoked, vaccination/rabies risk
Assess depth, location, and neurovascular function
Verify tetanus status
nursing: Assess for joint, tendon, or bone involvement
Document wound, source animal, and exposure history
keyOrders: Wound culture if signs of infection* if indicated
labs: None routine for low-risk bite
Wound culture if infected* if indicated
Defer extensive workup to ER if systemic illness
imaging: Plain X-ray if fracture, deep puncture, or retained tooth/foreign body suspected*
Defer advanced imaging to ER for deep-structure concern
meds: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg PO BID x 5-7 days (first-line prophylaxis/treatment)
Doxycycline + metronidazole or clindamycin if penicillin-allergic
Acetaminophen/ibuprofen for pain
Generally close cosmetically important wounds loosely; leave most puncture/cat bites open
reassess: Recheck neurovascular and tendon function
Confirm rabies and tetanus risk addressed
Reassess for early infection
disposition: Discharge home on antibiotics with wound care
Wound recheck in 24-48 hours
Transfer to ER or call 911 if deep hand/joint bite, signs of established infection/sepsis, high rabies risk needing PEP, or extensive injury
discharge: Elevate; keep wound clean; complete antibiotics
Return for redness, swelling, drainage, fever, or reduced movement
Watch for early infection (cat bites high risk)
followup: Wound check in 24-48 hours
PCP/health dept for rabies follow-up and animal observation
Hand surgery referral if function affected

redFlags: Deep hand, joint, or tendon involvement (high infection risk)
Established infection or systemic toxicity
High rabies risk requiring post-exposure prophylaxis
Crush injury or significant tissue loss
compliance: Urgent care manages low-risk bites; refer deep hand/joint bites and rabies PEP needs
Texas mandatory animal-bite reporting to local health authority
Update tetanus; assess rabies risk and arrange PEP/animal observation
EMTALA-style transfer to ER for high-risk or infected bites
